message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="lea-verou" color="pink" summary="CSS treatment details for stalled projects, processing outcomes, inbox badge">
Scott, great domain feedback. A few responses:

**Stalled project treatment**: The `project.is_stalled()` conditional in the template is clean. From a CSS perspective, the `.gtd-project__status--stalled` modifier would use `color: var(--color-warning)` or `color: var(--color-danger)` depending on how urgently we want to flag it. I'd lean toward warning (amber) rather than danger (red) -- a stalled project isn't broken, it just needs a next action. The warning color paired with text "No next action" gives two visual cues (color + text), satisfying our WCAG requirement that color is never the sole indicator.

**Processing flow auto-advance**: Good catch. After the `ProcessingOutcome` confirmation, the "Process next item" action is essentially a link to `/inbox/{next_id}/clarify`. If there are no more inbox items, it redirects to the dashboard with a success message. From a CSS standpoint, the confirmation state is just another swap of `.gtd-process__decision` -- maybe with a success background (`--color-success-light`) and the outcome summary ("Moved to Next Actions @computer"). Brief, scannable, then the user clicks through to the next item.

**Inbox count badge**: Agreed on muted rather than alarming. A non-empty inbox is the normal state in GTD -- the system is designed to accumulate captures throughout the day and process them during dedicated time. The count in the nav should be informational (`--color-fg-muted`), not a red alert badge. Steve Schoger proposed inline parenthesized counts ("Inbox (5)") which matches this intent perfectly.

**The composition insight**: You articulated it better than I did -- "these are all variations on the same core concepts, organized into different views by domain state." That's exactly why the CSS architecture works: one base component with modifiers, different metadata rendered per domain type. The CSS mirrors the domain model. When that happens, both are probably right.

Good alignment between domain and frontend. The templates will be straightforward -- Askama conditionals selecting which `.gtd-item__meta` content to render based on the domain type variant, with CSS handling the visual treatment through modifiers.
</teammate-message>

content.text:
Lea and I are aligned. One small note -- she suggested warning (amber) for stalled projects while Steve Schoger proposed danger (red) text with a warning border, specifically because the warning color alone fails WCAG AA contrast on white backgrounds. This is a design detail for Steve and Lea to resolve together, but Steve's contrast analysis was thorough and I'd lean toward his recommendation. The domain model is agnostic about the color -- it just provides `project.is_stalled()` and the CSS/design team decides the visual treatment.

All reviewer exchanges are complete. No outstanding items from my side. Standing by.
